title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 26 — Yuan Shao is Defeated and Loses a Leader / Guan Yu Abandons Rank and Wealth
text:
As the last chapter closedXuandehad been condemned to die.

He spoke up, however, and said, “Pray hear one word, Illustrious Sir, before you decide. I have lost sight of my brother since my misfortune atXu provinceand know not whetherYunchangbe dead or alive. There are many men in the world who resemble him. Is every red-faced man with a beard namedGuan Yu? Should you not rather seek some evidence?”

NowYuan Shaowas impulsive and facile by nature, and whenXuandespoke thus, he suddenly turned uponJu Shou, saying, “By wrongly regarding what you said, I nearly killed an innocent man.”

ThenYuan ShaorequestedXuandeonce more to resume his seat in the tent and advise him how to avengeYan Liang.

Soon from the lower end a voice was heard, saying, “Yan Liangand I were as brothers; how can I allow any other to avenge his death?”

The speaker was a man of middle height with a face like a unicorn, a famous leader fromHebei Region, namedWen Chou.

Yuan Shaowas pleased and said, “You are the only man who can do it. I will give you one hundred thousand troops, and you can cross theYellow River, and quickly smite that rebelCao Cao.”

“You cannot do it.Wen Chouwill fail,” saidJu Shou. “The proper course is to holdYan Ford, and detach a force toGuandu. If you cross the river rashly, and anything goes wrong, not a soul will return.”

Yuan Shaosaid, “That is always the way with you fellows, always delaying and taking the dash out of the soldiers. You put off today and postpone tomorrow until success has become impossible. Do you forget that promptitude is what the soldier honors?”

The adviser withdrew sadly, saying. “Superiors do not curb their ambitions: inferiors must strive to render service. Eternal is theYellow River, shall I cross it?”

Thereafter he feigned illness and went no more to the Council.

Xuandesaid, “I have received much kindness at your hands and have been unable to show my gratitude. I would accompany GeneralWen Chouthat I may repay your bounty, and also that I may hear news of my brother.”

Yuan Shaogladly consented and orderedWen Chouto share his command withXuande. But the former objected saying thatXuandehad been so often defeated that it would augur ill for success this time. He proposed to giveXuandecommand of the rear guard, and this being approved, three legions were told off underXuande’s special command to follow the main body.

The prowess displayed byLord Guanin the bold attack onYan LiangredoubledCao Cao’s respect for him and he memorialized the throne thatLord Guanreceive the title ofDistrict MarquisofHanshouand a seal was cast for him.

Just then came the unexpected news thatYuan Shao’s army had moved toward theYellow Riverand was in position aboveYan Ford.Cao Caofirst sent to transfer the inhabitants toXiheand then led out an army to opposeYuan Shao. He issued an order to face about, thus placing the rear companies in front. The commissariat wagons were also placed in the vanguard.

“What is this reversal for?” askedLü Qian.

Cao Caoreplied, “When the supplies are in rear they are liable to be plundered. So I have put them first.”

“But if you meet the enemy and they steal them?”

“Wait until the enemy appears; I shall know what to do.”

Lü Qianwas much exercised at this new move oftheChancellor’s. In the meantime the supply train moved along the river towardYan Ford. Presently the foremost troops raised a great shout andCao Caosent to see what it meant.

The messenger came back to sayWen Chou’s army was approaching, and the supply train had been abandoned and was at the mercy of the enemy.

ThereuponCao Caopointed to two mounds saying, “We will take refuge here for the present.”

All those near him hastened to the mounds. ThereCao Caoordered them all to loosen their dress, lay aside their breastplates and rest a time. The horsemen turned their steeds loose.

Wen Chou’s men approached under cover. As they drew near one after another the officers toldCao Caosaying, “The rebels are near: we ought to catch the horses and go back toBaima.”

But the adviser,Xun Yu, checked them saying, “These are a bait for the enemy: why retire?”

Cao Caoglanced across at him and said, “He understands; do not say anything.”

Now having got possession of the supply carts the enemy next came to seize the horses. By this time they had all broken ranks and were scattered, each man going his own way. Then suddenlyCao Caogave the order to go down from the mounds and smite them.

The surprise was complete.Wen Chou’s army was in confusion andCao Cao’s men surrounded them.Wen Choumade a stand, but those about him trampled each other down and he could do nothing but flee. And he fled.

Then standing on the top of a moundCao Caopointed to the flying leader calling out, “There is one of the most famous captains of the north; who can capture him?”

Zhang LiaoandXu Huangboth mounted and dashed after him, crying, “Wen Chou, do not run away!”

Looking round, the fugitive saw two pursuers and then he set aside his spear, took his bow and adjusted an arrow, which he shot atZhang Liao.

“Cease shooting, you rebel!” shoutedXu Huang.

Zhang Liaoducked his head and the shaft went harmlessly by, save that it carried away the tassel of his cap. He only pressed harder in pursuit. The next arrow however struck his horse in the head and the animal stumbled and fell, throwing his rider to the earth.

ThenWen Chouturned to come back.Xu Huang, whirling his battle ax, stood in his way to stop him. But he saw behind Wen several more horsemen coming to help him and as they would have been too many for him he fled. Wen pursued along the river bank. Suddenly he saw coming toward him with banners fluttering in the breeze, a small party of horse, and the leader carried a great sword.

“Stop!” criedYunchang, for it was he, and he attacked at once. At the third boutWen Chou’s heart failed him and he wheeled and fled, following the windings of the river. ButLord Guan’s steed was fast and soon caught up. One blow, and the haplessWen Choufell.

WhenCao Caosaw from the mound that the leader of the enemy had fallen, he gave the signal for a general onset and many of the northmen were driven into the river. And the carts with supplies and all the horses were quickly recovered.

NowLord Guan, at the head of a few horsemen, was thrusting here and striking there at the moment whenXuande, with the three reserve legions, appeared on the battle field. At once they told him that the red-faced, long-bearded warrior was there and had slainWen Chou. He hastily pressed forward to try to get a look at the warrior. He saw across the river a large body of horse and the banners bore the words “Guan Yunchang,District MarquisofHanshou.”

“Then it is my brother and he is really withCao Cao,” saidXuande, secretly thanking God that he was safe.

He made an attempt to wait about until he could call toLord Guan, but a great mass ofCao Cao’s soldiers came rushing down and he was forced to retire.

Yuan Shao, with reinforcements, arrived atGuanduand built a stockade.

Two advisers,Guo TuandShen Pei, went in to see him and said, “Again that fellowGuan Yuhas been in the battle. He killedWen Chou.Liu Beipretends ignorance of him.”

Their master was angry and railed atXuande. “The long-eared rebel! How dare he do such a thing?”

SoonXuandeappeared; againYuan Shaoordered him out to instant execution.

“What crime have I committed?” askedXuande.

“You sent your brother to slay one of my generals. Is that no crime?”

“Pray let me explain before I die.Cao Caohated me and has always done so. Now he has found out where I am and, fearing that I may help you, has got my brother to destroy your two generals, feeling sure that when you heard of it you would be angry and put me to death. You cannot fail to see this.”

“What he says is sensible,” saidYuan Shao, “and you two nearly brought on me the reproach of injuring the good.”

He ordered his attendants to retire and askedXuandeto come and sit by him.Xuandecame saying, “I am deeply thankful, Illustrious Sir, for your great kindness, for which I can never be sufficiently grateful. Now I desire to send some confidential messenger with a secret letter to my brother to tell him where I am, and I am sure he will come without a moment’s delay. He will help you to destroyCao Caoto make up for having destroyed your two officers. Do you approve of this?”

“If I gotYunchang, he would be ten times better than the two men I have lost,” repliedYuan Shao.

SoXuandeprepared a letter. But there was no one to take it.Yuan Shaoordered the army to withdraw toWuyang, where they made a large camp. For some time nothing was done.

ThenCao CaosentXiahou Dunto defend the strategical point atGuanduwhile he led the bulk of the army back tothe capital. There he gave many banquets in honor of the services ofLord Guanand then he toldLü Qianthat putting the supplies in the front of the army had been meant as a bait to draw the enemy to destruction.

“OnlyXun Yuunderstood that,” saidCao Caoin conclusion.

Every one present praised his ingenuity. Even while the banquet was proceeding there arrived news of a rising ofYellow Turbanrebels atRunan. They were very strong andCao Honghad been defeated in several engagements. Now he begged for help.

Lord Guan, hearing this said, “I should like to have the opportunity of performing some service by destroying these rebels.”

“You have already rendered noble services for which you have not been properly requited. I could hardly trouble you again,” saidCao Cao.

“I have been idle too long; I shall get ill,” saidLord Guan.

Cao Caoencouraged him to go, and assigned him fifty thousand troops along withYu JinandYu Jinto serve as captains. They were to leave soon.

ThenXun Yusaid privily to his master, “He always cherishes the idea of returning toXuande. He will leave you if he hears any news. Do not let him go on this expedition.”

“If he does well this time I will not let him go into battle again,” saidCao Cao.

In due time the force led byLord Guandrew near the rebels and made their camp. One night, just outside his camp, two spies were caught and taken in toLord Guan, who in one of them recognizedSun Qian. The attendants being dismissedLord Guanquestioned him.

“After we lost sight of each other I have heard not a word of you, what are you doing here?”

“After I escaped I drifted hither and thither until I had the good fortune to reachRunanandLiu Pitook me in. But why are you withCao Cao, General? And where are your sisters-in-law? Are they well?”

Lord Guantold him all that had happened.

“I have heard lately thatXuandeis withYuan Shao. I would have liked to go and join him, but I have not found a convenient opportunity. Now the two men I am with have taken the side ofYuan ShaoagainstCao Cao. By good luck you were coming here so I got command of a small party of scouts to be able to see you and tell you. Presently our two leaders will pretend to be defeated and you, and the two ladies, can go over toYuan Shao. And you will see your brother.”

“Since he is there I certainly must go at once to see him. But it is a misfortune that I have slain two ofYuan Shao’s generals. I fear things are not in my favor,” saidLord Guan.

“Let me go first and see how the land lie; I will come back and tell you.”

“I would risk a myriad deaths to see my brother,” saidLord Guan. “But I must go to say farewell toCao Cao.”

Sun Qianwas sent away that night and next dayLord Guanled out his men to offer battle.Gong Du, in armor, went out to the front of the line of battle andLord Guansaid, “You people, why have you risen against the government?”

“Why do you blame us when you have turned your back on your own lord?” repliedGong Du.

“How have I turned my back on my lord?”

“Liu Xuandeis withYuan Benchuand you are withCao Cao, what is that?”

Lord Guancould not reply, but he whirled round his sword and rode forward.Gong Dufled, andLord Guanfollowed. Some distance cleared,Gong Duturned and said, “I cannot forget my old chief’s kindness. Now attack as soon as you can, and I will give up the defenses.”

Lord Guanunderstood and urged on his troops. The leaders of the rebels pretended they were worsted, and they all scattered. SoRunanwas taken. Having pacified the people,Lord Guanquickly led his army back tothe capital, where he was met byCao Cao, where he was congratulated on his success and feasted.

When this was all overLord Guanwent to the dwelling of his sisters-in-law to pay his respects at their gate.

“Have you been able to get any news ofImperial UncleLiu in your two expeditions?” asked theLady Gan.

“None,” repliedLord Guan.

As he retired from the door he heard sounds of bitter weeping within.

“Alas he is dead,” said they. “Our brother-in-law thinks we shall be greatly distressed and hides the truth from us.”

One of the old soldiers, who acted as guard, hearing the sounds of perpetual grief, took pity on them and said, “Do not weep, ladies; your lord is withYuan ShaoinHebei Region.”

“How do you know that?” said they.

“I went out withGeneralGuanYu, and one of the soldiers told me.”

The two ladies summonedLord Guanand reproached him saying, “Imperial UncleLiu never betrayed you and yet you remain here enjoying the bounty ofCao Caoand forgetting the old times. And you tell us falsehoods.”

Lord Guanbowed his head. “My brother really is inHebei Region, but I dared not tell you, lest it should become known. Something must be done but done carefully and it needs time.”

“Brother-in-law, you should hasten,” said theLady Gan.Lord Guanwithdrew feeling that he must evolve some scheme of departure without further loss of time. It caused him much uneasiness.

Yu Jin, having learned thatXuandewas in the north, toldCao Cao, who at once sentZhang Liaoto find outLord Guan’s intentions.

Zhang Liaoentered jauntily and congratulatedLord Guan, saying, “They tell me you obtained news of your brother in the battlefield. I felicitate you.”

“My lord was there indeed, but I met him not. I see nothing to be glad about.”

“Is there any difference between the relationship of you two, and that of any other two brothers?”

Lord Guanreplied, “You and I stand in the relationship of friends.Xuandeand I are friends and brothers beside, and prince and minister in addition to both. Our relationship cannot be discussed in usual terms.”

“Well, now that you know where your brother is, are you going to him?”

“How can I go back on what I said before? I am sure you will explain fully totheChancellor.”

Zhang Liaowent back and told his master who said, “I must find a way to keep him here.”

WhileLord Guanwas pondering over his difficulties, they told him that a friend had come inquiring for him. The visitor was introduced, butLord Guandid not recognize him.

“Who are you?” askedLord Guan.

“I amChen ZhenofNanyang, in service ofYuan Shao.”

In great perturbation,Lord Guansent away the attendants and, now alone with his guest, asked, “Is there is some special reason for your visit?”

For reply the newcomer drew out a letter and handed it to his host, who recognized that it was from his brotherXuande.

I, the writer, and you, Sir, pledged ourselves in the Peach Garden to die together. Why then are we apart and yet alive, our kindly feelings destroyed, our sense of right outraged? Surely you desire to obtain fame and acquire riches and honor by offering my head as a crowning act of merit. More might be said but I await your commands with great anxiety.

Lord Guanfinished the letter with a bitter cry.

“I always wanted to find my brother, but I did not know where he was. How can he think such evil of me?” said he.

“Xuandelooks for you very eagerly, and if you are still bound by the old pledge you should go quickly,” said the messenger.

“Anyone born into the world without the one essential virtue of sincerity is no true man. I came here openly and can go in no other way. Now will I write a letter which I will ask you to bear to my brother, that as soon as I can take leave ofCao CaoI will bring the ladies and come to him.”

“But what ifCao Caorefuses to let you go?” saidChen Zhen.

“Then would I rather die. I will not remain here.”

“Then, Sir, quickly write your letter and relieve your brother from his anxiety.”

SoLord Guanwrote:

I, the humble one, know full well that a man of principle does not betray and a man of loyalty despises death. I have been a student in my youth and know somewhat of the proprieties. I sigh and weep at the memory of the fraternal affection that madeYangjue AiandZuo Botaodie rather than separate.

I was in charge ofXiapibut the place lacked provision and there was no help. I would have fought to the death but there was on my shoulders the responsibility for my sisters-in-law. Wherefore I had to take care of my body lest I betrayed my trust. And so I made a prisoner of myself, hoping to find a way of release.

I heard of you lately inRunan. I must, however, bid farewell toCao Caoand bring the ladies with me when I come. May I perish, victim to the superhuman powers, if I have harbored any traitorous thought.

Pencil and paper are poor substitutes for what I would say, but I look to see you soon.

Chen Zhenwith this missive, andLord Guanwent to tell the women. Then he proceeded totheChancellor’s palace to say farewell. ButCao Caoknew what he was coming for, and he found at the gate he found posted a message intimating that no one could be received. So he had to return. However, he bade his own few soldiers prepare to start at any moment. He also gave orders that every gift received fromCao Caowas to be left in the quarters. Nothing was to be taken.

Next day he again proceeded to the palace to say farewell to his patron, but again found the board hanging there to show there was no admission. So it was several times; he could never enter. Then he went to seeZhang Liao, but he was indisposed.

“So he will not let me go,” thoughtLord Guan. “But I am going, and I shall wait no longer.”

So he wrote this letter:

As a young man I entered the service of theImperial Uncle, and pledged myself to share his fortunes. Heaven and Earth witnessed this oath. When I lost the city I made three requests which you granted.

Now I hear my brother is withYuan Shaoand I, remembering our pledge, cannot but go to him. Though your bounty is great I forget not the bond of the past, wherefore I write this letter of farewell trusting that when you have read it you will be content for me to postpone to another season the proof of my gratitude.

Lord Guansealed and sent it to the Palace. Then he deposited in the treasury of his dwelling all the gold and silver he had received, suspended his seal of marquis in the reception hall, and left, taking his sisters-in-law with him in a carriage. He rode Red Hare and carriedGreen Dragonat his hand. With a small escort of guards, formerly of his command, he left by the north gate.

The wardens would have stopped him, but he frightened them with a fierce shout. Having got out, he told the escort to go in front with the carriage while he would remain behind to guard against pursuit. So they pushed the carriage toward the high road.

In the cityLord Guan’s letter reachedtheChancellorwhile he was consulting about what to do. He read it and exclaimed, “So he has left!”

Then the warden of the gate came to report thatLord Guanhad forced his way out, and was gone with a carriage, a horse, and a score of guards. Next came the servants from his house to report that he had left, taking no treasure, nor any of the waiting maids. Everything was left in the house. Even his seal was there. His only escort was few men left of his original command.

Suddenly from the assembly of men rose a voice, saying, “With three companies of mailed horse, I will bring him back alive.”

Their eyes turned to the speaker, who was GeneralCai Yang.

On the dragon’s cave he turns his back,

But numberless wolves infest his track.

What came of this offer to pursue will be seen in the next chapter.
